The reviewer has written a hoax/creative-fiction review — an invented ninth-century adab manuscript, an invented Irwin "translation" of it, and a real-sounding critical apparatus on top — that itself enacts the book's nested frame (translator recovering a sun-bleached manuscript). It is a literary performance, not a report, and on those terms it is dazzling. The structure (al-Rabahi's life and Basra's 871 sack -> Irwin's life and Basra's 1991 destruction -> the same parable read twice, once for the Zanj Rebellion and once for the Gulf War -> the absurdity thesis -> the reviewer's own Dawkins-memetic answer) is meticulously parallel and pays off. The invented adab prose and poetry are convincingly pastiched, and the central tension — that preserving culture is both necessary and absurd, and that a "cultivated person" is just substrate fully colonized by his culture's memes — is a real, non-obvious idea well argued. Originality is off the chart; this is a take no one else will have. Engagement is high though the deliberately dry, scholarly register and the heavy Section IV syllogism slow the middle. The big risk is fairness/accuracy: the review never openly admits the book is fabricated, and a credulous reader could be deceived (the winking opening and footnotes are the only tells). For a contest that prizes honest representation of a real book, that is a genuine vulnerability — but the conceit is the achievement, not a cheat, and it engages the strongest version of its own ideas. A standout, but its gimmick caps how broadly it will land.
strongest: The double-reading of a single invented parable — first decoded against the ninth-century Zanj Rebellion, then re-decoded line-by-line against the 1991 Gulf War (the "black rain" as burning Kuwaiti oil wells, "until the very plains contract" as the Highway of Death) — is a brilliant demonstration of the historical-rhyme thesis, and the fact that the reviewer authored both the parable and both readings makes the payoff structurally self-proving.
weakest: It reviews a non-existent book without ever clearly disclosing the fabrication, which strains the contest's fairness/accuracy expectation and risks deceiving rather than delighting the reader; the dense formal-logic argument in Section IV is also the driest stretch and slightly over-explains a point the parables already made.

title: Venomous Lumpsucker by Ned Beauman
verdict: Yes, shortlist-worthy: it makes a satirical climate novel feel urgent and intellectually live by reframing it as a book about ideas-built-out, and the prose itself argues for the book's worth.
strongest: The central thesis that the novel takes contemporary moral propositions (markets as moral allocators, hedonic utilitarianism) and faithfully builds them out, leaving the ethical work to the reader, is genuinely illuminating and reframes the book as something other than ordinary climate fiction.
weakest: It leans heavily on retelling the plot and Resaint's reasoning at length, so the reviewer's own argument, while present and good, occupies less of the wordcount than the book's content does, and the closing "black holes already exist" turn is more atmospheric than incisive.

title: Violence and the Sacred
verdict: A finalist-caliber review that refuses to merely summarize Girard and instead uses him as scaffolding for an original argument. It opens with three arresting ethnographic vignettes (pharmakos, ixiptla, Meriah) that pose a real puzzle, explains mimetic theory and the scapegoat mechanism clearly, and then does the thing most reviews never attempt: in Part Two it assembles a rival synthesis of the sacred (Huizinga, Boyer, Durkheim, Douglas, Rapaport) and locates exactly what Girard uniquely explains, namely the paradoxically honored-and-punished victim. Part Three advances the author's own thesis, that the sacred declined in proportion to our willingness to inflict and suffer violence, and scores a clean hit by showing Girard leaned on the discredited hydraulic/catharsis model, which undermines his apocalyptic predictions. The voice is confident and funny ("an irresistible urge to shoot spitballs at the back of his head," "sacred carcinization"), and it is consistently fair, steelmanning Girard and flagging its own speculation in footnotes. The main cost is length and drift: Part Three balloons into a broad lament on liberalism, democracy, capitalism, and biopolitics that travels far from the book, and the breadth sometimes leans on name-density to carry the argument. The closing thesis is elegant but asserted more than proven. Still well above the field.
strongest: The synthetic move in Part Two: instead of only critiquing Girard, it builds a competing multi-source theory of the sacred and then pinpoints the one phenomenon (the victim as both poison and cure, honored and punished) that the scapegoat mechanism explains better than its rivals. That is genuine intellectual work, not reporting.
weakest: Part Three's sprawl. The general indictment of liberalism, democracy, capitalism, sovereignty, and biopolitics is engaging but drifts from the book under review and occasionally substitutes a parade of thinkers for tight argument, leaving the central thesis (violence as the litmus of the sacred) more asserted than demonstrated.

title: We Present Television (ed. ~1940 essay collection)
verdict: Yes, shortlist it: an obscure 1940 book turned into a confident, original argument that "Television" was always a metaphysical force of viewing-from-a-distance whose prophets accidentally described the internet.
strongest: It reframes a forgotten technical primer into a genuinely non-obvious thesis—the TV set as mere shackle on a larger "Unseen Force"—and earns it by showing how the authors' own predictions land in 2026.
weakest: It runs long and the middle "tour of the studio" occasionally drifts toward cataloguing before the prophecy section reignites the argument.
